medical_safety_note: This JSON is an accessibility/life-impact catalogue derived from the attached PDF and cautious general health-literacy reasoning. It is not a diagnostic tool, treatment plan, or medical advice. Individual impact varies, especially with comorbid conditions, environment, support, money, housing, work demands, flare cycles, and care access.

entry_schema.id: stable slug unique within this JSON
entry_schema.name: condition, limitation, concept, or mobility aid name
entry_schema.source_group: category used by the source PDF
entry_schema.source_page: first page where the source term appears, when found
entry_schema.source_excerpt: short extracted context from the PDF, when available
entry_schema.life_limitations: ways it may limit daily living, relationships, work, safety, mobility, communication, or energy
entry_schema.possible_life_improvements_or_supports: ways recognition, accommodations, treatment, tools, community, or the aid itself may slightly improve life/functioning
entry_schema.support_or_accommodation_ideas: non-prescriptive supports to discuss with appropriate professionals/support people
entry_schema.spoon_energy_notes: how it may affect planning under Spoon Theory / energy pacing
entry_schema.individual_variability_note: reminder that impact differs by person and day

source_specific_note: The source explains that hormones regulate many bodily functions, so endocrine imbalance can have varied body-wide effects.

entries.name: Adrenal Insufficiency
entries.source_excerpt: eatment options for many types of metabolic and endocrine disorders, including: Adrenal Insufficiency Adrenal glands, located on top of the kidneys, produce various hormones. Adrenal insufficiency occurs when the adrenal glands do not produce sufficient amounts of steroid hormones — primarily cortisol, which regulates sodium conservation, potassium secretion, and water retention. Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is a group of inherited genetic disorders that affect the adrenal glands. A person
life_limitations: Hormone imbalance can affect multiple body systems, so symptoms may interfere with energy, mood, body temperature, appetite/weight, sleep, concentration, strength, blood pressure, and daily reliability.
Can limit stamina, stress tolerance, hydration/salt balance, illness recovery, and safety during physical stress because cortisol regulation is impaired.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Diagnosis and monitoring can slightly improve life by making symptoms explainable, treatable, and easier to plan around.
A steroid/emergency plan and predictable medication access can improve safety and confidence.

entries.name: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
entries.source_excerpt: which regulates sodium conservation, potassium secretion, and water retention. Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is a group of inherited genetic disorders that affect the adrenal glands. A person with CAH lacks one of the enzymes the adrenal glands use to produce hormones that help regulate metabolism, the immune system, blood pressure, and other essential functions. entries.name: Hyperaldosteronism
entries.source_excerpt: e metabolism, the immune system, blood pressure, and other essential functions. Hyperaldosteronism Hyperaldosteronism is a disease where the adrenal glands make too much aldosterone, a hormone that stimulates absorption of sodium by the kidneys and helps to regulate water and salt balance in the body. When too much aldosterone is produced, this leads to hypertension (high blood pressure) and low blood potassium levels. entries.name: Pituitary Disorders
entries.source_excerpt: res. Falls in people with osteoporosis can lead to serious health consequences. Pituitary Disorders Pituitary tumors are abnormal growths that develop in your pituitary gland. Some pituitary tumors result in too many of the hormones that regulate important functions of the body; others can cause the pituitary gland to produce lower levels of hormones. Most pituitary tumors are noncancerous (benign) growths (adenomas). Adenomas remain in your pituitary gland or surrounding tissues and don't spread to other parts of your body. Thyroid D
life_limitations: Hormone imbalance can affect multiple body systems, so symptoms may interfere with energy, mood, body temperature, appetite/weight, sleep, concentration, strength, blood pressure, and daily reliability.
Can affect many hormone-controlled functions such as growth, fertility, lactation, thyroid/adrenal function, energy, headaches, vision, and mood depending on the hormone involved.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Diagnosis and monitoring can slightly improve life by making symptoms explainable, treatable, and easier to plan around.
Specialist monitoring can connect seemingly unrelated symptoms and guide hormone replacement or other treatment.

entries.name: Thyroid Disorders
entries.source_excerpt: tary gland or surrounding tissues and don't spread to other parts of your body. Thyroid Disorders The thyroid gland is an endocrine gland that is located in the front of the neck. This gland produces thyroid hormones that primarily influence the body's metabolism and protein synthesis. Abnormal cell growth in the thyroid gland can lead to thyroid cancer. The thyroid also can be affected by a variety of diseases, including: Hypothyroidism: Hypothyroidism is an underactive thyroid gland, and the thyroid gland can't make enough thyroid h

entries.name: Hypothyroidism
entries.source_excerpt: d cancer. The thyroid also can be affected by a variety of diseases, including: Hypothyroidism: Hypothyroidism is an underactive thyroid gland, and the thyroid gland can't make enough thyroid hormone to keep the body running normally. Individuals are hypothyroid if they have too little thyroid hormone in the blood. Common causes are autoimmune disease, surgical removal of the thyroid, and radiation treatment. Hyperthyroidism: Hyperthyroidism is a condition in which the thyroid gland is overactive and makes excessive amounts of thyroid
